notes: Use these as soft priors only. Live audio analysis should override title-based hints quickly.

rhythm.notes: Brief rhythmic joke usually played during 1970 tuning.
Straight-ahead rock shuffle with aggressive backbeat.
Primal Dead era; heavy polyrhythmic influence from Hart/Kreutzmann.
Highly distinctive 'laid back' pocket; avoid rushing the 16th subdivisions.
Often performed without instruments; difficult to track via PCM.

entries.title: Are You Lonely For Me Baby
entries.canonical_title: Are You Lonely For Me Baby

rhythm.notes: Freddie Scott cover; heavy JGB R&B swing.
Double-time feel often occurs in the final crescendo.
Slow, complex vocal timing; instrumental track is minimal.
Mickey Hart/Drums era exploration; polyrhythmic focus.

entries.title: Baby Blue
aliases: It's All Over Now, Baby Blue

rhythm.notes: Dylan cover; steady, rhythmic breathing in the performance.
Dylan cover; 6/8 time signature with heavy emphasis on the 'one'.
Rare Bobby & Midnites/GD crossover; very vertical 80s rhythm.

entries.title: Beat It On Down the Line
entries.canonical_title: Beat It On Down the Line

rhythm.notes: Features 'n' count intro; syncopated snare throughout.
Rare late-80s Garcia ballad; rhythm is very understated.
Consistent upbeat feel; avoid phase-locking to early guitar stabs.
Pigpen-era cornerstone; shuffle-blues feel.
Driving bluegrass-inspired rock shuffle.
Johnny Cash cover; pulse often feels like a fast 2/4.
Deeply swung 8th note feel; jam sections often expand to 7/8 in modern variants.
Very slow tempo; focus on the kick-snare relationship for stability.
Slow blues feel; crescendo into bridge is rhythmically denser.
Syncopated acoustic guitar accents the '2' and '4'.
Brent Mydland era staple; very driving vertical energy.
Stable tempo; focus on the constant 8th note strumming pulse.
Gentle shuffle subdivision; avoid latching onto melodic lead lines.
Highly rhythmic piano and guitar interplay; very strong 'backpocket' feel.
Late era Garcia track; very defined, vertical rhythmic structure.
Half-time feel in the drums; focus on the lead guitar phrasing for cues.
Classic locomotive rhythm; snare ghost notes are critical.
Heavy use of cross-sticking and ride cymbal wash; jam section accelerates.
JGB cornerstone; extremely tight, syncopated 16th note feel.
Early primal Dead; feedback-driven rhythmic assault.
Jerry Garcia Band 70s-80s cover; vertical R&B pulse.
Jerry Garcia solo/acoustic era; standard jazz swing.
Phil Lesh 1994-95 song; airy, loosely defined pulse.
Counterpoint rhythm is key; usually segues into I Know You Rider.
Pigpen-era high-swing shuffle.
Extremely fragile pulse; avoids strong backbeat until the end.
Rare 1968 track; unusual 3/4 or 6/8 meter for the era.
Distinctive lead-in on the '1'; high energy acoustic-to-electric feel.
Very wide pocket; focus on the kick for timing.
90s era staple; heavy use of MIDI percussion and 'greasy' swing.
Lazy but precise shuffle; difficult to track because of fluid vocal timing.
Heavy 'one-drop' reggae influence; transition into 7/8 jam at the end.
Rapid-fire acoustic/electric hybrid rhythm; polyrhythmic accents.
70s era is disco-fied; 60s era is more Motown stomp.
Classic country rhythm; focus on alternating bass notes.
Tempo is a guideline; rhythm often breaks down into free-form exploration.
Rigid, upbeat tempo typical of early 80s rockers.
Late-era masterpiece; percussion is cinematic and sparse.
Crescendo jams in the 80s/90s can double in rhythmic density.
Very slow, minor-key blues feel.
Particularly strong in acoustic sets; bouncy rhythmic pocket.
Stable tempo; focus on the repetitive snare patterns.
Avoid locking to the pedal steel glissando; drum pulse is very square.
Aoxomoxoa-era complexity; many stop-starts in the rhythm.
High energy set closer; vertical and driving.
Phase-locking impossible; focus on transient density rather than steady BPM.
Heavy swing; focus on the piano's left-hand rhythmic bounce.
90s era Weir song; very vertical, almost industrial blues pulse.
Brent Mydland era; straightforward, pop-inflected rock pulse.
Pigpen-led Workingman's Dead era; heavy rhythmic focus on the groove.
Strictly 3/4 time; avoid locking to the 4/4 backbeat if drums are present.
Strict 7/4 time; pulse feels like 3+4 or 4+3 depending on the era.
90s era Weir song; very heavy and rhythmic guitar riffs.
Los Lobos cover; driving, rhythmic accordion/guitar interplay.
73-74 era is fast and light; 80s-90s era is slower and funkier.
Brent era rocker; driving, non-swing rhythm.
Folk traditional; very steady, predictable tempo.
The 'bubble' rhythm is key; heavy 16th note syncopation in the bass.
Difficult counter-rhythms in the intro; main song is very vertical.
Shakedown Street rarity; unusually 'produced' and vertical rhythm.
Consistent, hypnotic groove; rhythmic transients on the 'and' of each beat.
Garcia/Grisman style hyper-fast acoustic pulse.
Rare early-80s Bob Weir rocker; driving energy.
Van Morrison cover; relentless E-A-D repetitive pulse.
Pigpen centerpiece; gritty, mid-tempo blues swing.
Features a complex, fast 16th note guitar intro; very strong backbeat.
Pigpen-era Otis Redding cover; very strong rhythmic focus.
Pocket is everything; avoid rushing the vocal delivery.
Uses complex 3/4 or 6/8 patterns with heavy syncopation.
Aggressive 80s rocker; vertical pulse with minimal swing.
Tempo is extremely slow; pulse is largely defined by vocal phrasing.
Rare early cover; features a distinctive mid-60s dance floor pulse.

rhythm.notes: Acoustic era favorite; gentle but firm steady pulse.
Very brisk tempo; focus on the driving 8th note subdivisions.
Starts slow/folk; jam sections accelerate into high energy rock shuffle.
Old & In The Way era; strictly bluegrass timing.
Bobby cover; very punchy, vertical R&B shuffle.

Late-era Garcia ballad; distinctively 'lazy' swing feel.
90s era upbeat rocker; very stable, vertical pulse.
Ultra-heavy blues swing; often deliberately slow and dragging.
Brent Mydland rare track; features a tense, driving rhythm.
Classic Bob Weir polka; pulse is strictly alternating 1-2-1-2.
Wilson Pickett cover; high energy R&B pulse.
Energetic Garcia solo track; relentless forward momentum.
Pigpen cornerstone; very heavy 'chug' on the subdivision.
Rare Mars Hotel era rocker; very rigid and non-swing.
Donovan cover turned Allman-esque jam; features loose, melodic drumming.
Relentless blues shuffle; the slide guitar creates sharp rhythmic transients.
Pigpen-era soul-blues; features a very distinct shuffle pulse.
Traditional cover; often features a raw, primal rhythmic focus.
Fast, driving acoustic traditional.
Terrapin-era rocker; driving, vertical pulse with very little swing.
